---
title: "LGBTQ+ Representation in Television Series"

website:
  title: "LGBTQ+ Representation in Television Series"

format:
  html:
    toc: false
    theme:
      - custom.scss
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Code anzeigen"

execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false

jupyter: python3
---
<a href="#" class="back-to-top"
   onclick="window.scrollTo({ top: 0, behavior: 'smooth' }); return false;">
  <img src="assets/up-arrow.png" alt="Back to top">
</a>

<div class="section-nav">
  <a href="#introduction">Intro</a>
  <a href="#research-question">Question</a>
  <a href="#pipeline-overview">Pipeline</a>
  <a href="#dataset-overview">Analysis</a>
  <a href="#key-findings">Findings</a>
  <a href="#conclusion">Conclusion</a>
</div>

<div id="top" class="hero-card">

<h1>
LGBTQ+<br>
<span class="outline-text">Representation</span><br>
In Television Series
</h1>

</div>

<div class="authors-card">
ARINA RUKINA
<span>•</span>
LEILA OSMAN
<span>•</span>
NADJA SALEH
<span>•</span>
RENATA FIGUEROA
</div>


# Introduction {#introduction}
<section id="introduction" class="intro-layout">

<div class="intro-text">

LGBTQ+ representation in television and streaming media has become increasingly visible in recent years. Television series not only reflect social developments but also influence public perception of gender and sexuality. Because of this, analyzing LGBTQ+ representation in media is relevant for understanding broader cultural trends.

This project investigates LGBTQ+ characters in dramatic television series from the 2020s. The project combines automated data extraction, data cleaning, keyword-based label detection, and manual review in order to build a structured dataset of LGBTQ+ television characters.

The project also demonstrates how computational methods and reproducible data pipelines can be used for media and gender studies research.
</div>

<div class="intro-image">
<img src="assets/rainbow.png" alt="Rainbow illustration">
</div>

</section>

# Research Question {#research-question}
<div class="research-card">

<p>
The central research question of this project is:
</p>

<blockquote>
Which LGBTQ+ identities are represented most frequently in dramatic television series of the 2020s?
</blockquote>

<p>
Additional questions include:
</p>

<ul>
  <li>Which identities appear most often?</li>
  <li>Which identities are underrepresented?</li>
  <li>How effective is automatic identity classification using text-based methods?</li>
</ul>

</div>

# Data Source {#data-source}
<div class="data-grid">

<div class="data-source-card">

<p class="source-label">Source Platform</p>

<p class="source-value">
<a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_dramatic_television_series_with_LGBTQ_characters:_2020s" target="_blank">
Wikipedia ↗
</a>
</p>

<p class="source-label">Source Dataset</p>

<p class="source-value">
List of dramatic television series with LGBTQ characters: 2020s
</p>

<p class="source-label">Extracted Variables</p>

<ul>
  <li>Year</li>
  <li>Series</li>
  <li>Network</li>
  <li>Character</li>
  <li>Actor</li>
  <li>Notes</li>
</ul>

</div>

<div class="data-collection-card">

<p class="source-label">Collection Method</p>

<p class="source-value">
Wikipedia Table Extraction
</p>

<p>
Raw data was extracted from the Wikipedia table and converted into a structured CSV dataset.
</p>

<p class="source-label">Output Dataset</p>

<pre><code>lgbtq_series_wiki_table.csv</code></pre>

</div>

</div>


# Project Structure {#project-structure}
<div class="structure-card">

<p class="source-label">

<p>
The project repository is organized into four main components:
</p>

<blockquote>
● <strong>data</strong> – raw and processed datasets<br>
● <strong>scripts</strong> – Python scripts for extraction, cleaning, enrichment, and visualization<br>
● <strong>visualizations</strong> – generated charts and figures<br>
● <strong>quarto</strong> – report files and styling
</blockquote>

```text

Projekt_GenderData/

├── data/
│   ├── raw/
│   └── processed/

├── scripts/

├── visualizations/

├── quarto/

├── requirements.txt

└── README.md
```
</div>

# Pipeline Overview {#pipeline-overview}

<div class="pipeline-card">

<p>
The project follows a structured data pipeline that transforms raw Wikipedia data into a cleaned, enriched, and reproducible dataset for analysis.
</p>

<blockquote>
From raw data collection to final visualization, each step of the workflow was implemented and documented in Python.
</blockquote>

<div class="pipeline-flow">

<div class="pipeline-step red">
WIKIPEDIA DATASET
</div>

<div class="pipeline-arrow">↓</div>

<div class="pipeline-step orange">
DATA EXTRACTION
</div>

<div class="pipeline-arrow">↓</div>

<div class="pipeline-step yellow">
DATA CLEANING
</div>

<div class="pipeline-arrow">↓</div>

<div class="pipeline-step green">
IDENTITY DETECTION
</div>

<div class="pipeline-arrow">↓</div>

<div class="pipeline-step blue">
RELATIONSHIP INFERENCE
</div>

<div class="pipeline-arrow">↓</div>

<div class="pipeline-step purple">
MANUAL REVIEW
</div>

<div class="pipeline-arrow">↓</div>

<div class="pipeline-step red">
FINAL DATASET
</div>

<div class="pipeline-arrow">↓</div>

<div class="pipeline-step orange">
VISUALIZATIONS
</div>

<div class="pipeline-arrow">↓</div>

<div class="pipeline-step blue">
QUARTO REPORT
</div>

</div>

<p>
Each stage produces an output that serves as the input for the next stage, ensuring a transparent and reproducible workflow.
</p>

<p>
The pipeline combines automated processing with manual validation in order to improve data quality while maintaining reproducibility.
</p>

</div>

# Data Cleaning & Data Enrichment {#data-cleaning}

<div class="pipeline-grid">

<div class="cleaning-card">

<p>
Before the analysis could begin, the dataset needed to be cleaned and standardized.
</p>

<blockquote>
The raw Wikipedia table contained formatting inconsistencies, citation markers, and additional text elements that were not useful for the analysis.
</blockquote>

<p>
The following preprocessing steps were performed:
</p>

<ul>
  <li>Removal of Wikipedia citation markers such as [1]</li>
  <li>Removal of bracketed information such as (season 2)</li>
  <li>Cleaning and normalization of character names</li>
  <li>Standardization of text formatting</li>
  <li>Removal of unnecessary whitespace</li>
</ul>

<p>
For example:
</p>

<pre><code>TK Strand[1] (season 2)</code></pre>

<p>
became:
</p>

```{python}
import re

name = "TK Strand[1] (season 2)"

name = re.sub(r"\[.*?\]", "", name)
name = re.sub(r"\(.*?\)", "", name)

print(name)
```
</div>

<div class="enrichment-card">

<p>
After cleaning the dataset, the next step involved enriching the data with LGBTQ+ identity labels.
</p>

<blockquote>
The project used a rule-based text classification approach implemented in Python.
</blockquote>

<p>
The script analyzed the content of the <code>Notes</code> column and searched for keywords and relationship descriptions associated with LGBTQ+ identities.
</p>

<p>
Regular expressions (regex) were used to detect patterns such as:
</p>

<ul>
  <li>gay</li>
  <li>lesbian</li>
  <li>bisexual</li>
  <li>transgender</li>
  <li>queer</li>
  <li>non-binary</li>
  <li>pansexual</li>
  <li>asexual</li>
</ul>

<p>
For example:
</p>

<pre><code>Rachel and Dana are in a romantic relationship.
X and Y are lovers.</code></pre>

</div>

</div>

# Identity Detection {#identity-detection}
<div class="identity-card">

After cleaning and preparing the dataset, the next step involved automatically identifying LGBTQ+ identities.

The project used a rule-based classification system implemented in Python. Character descriptions contained in the `Notes` column were analyzed using keyword matching and regular expressions (regex).

The system searched for explicit references to LGBTQ+ identities such as:

- Gay
- Lesbian
- Bisexual
- Transgender
- Non-binary
- Pansexual
- Asexual

For example, descriptions containing terms such as:

```text
The character is gay.
The character is bisexual.
The character identifies as non-binary.
```

could be automatically assigned to the corresponding category.

```{python}
import re

text = "The character is bisexual."

if re.search(r"\bbisexual\b", text.lower()):
    print("Bisexual")
```

<blockquote>
The detection process was implemented through predefined keyword patterns and regular expressions. This approach allowed a large portion of the dataset to be classified automatically before manual review became necessary.
</blockquote>

The automatic classification stage significantly reduced the amount of manual work required during the later phases of the project.
</div>

# Relationship Inference {#relationship-inference}
<div class="relationship-card">

Not all LGBTQ+ identities were explicitly stated in the character descriptions.

To improve the classification process, an additional relationship inference step was implemented.

The system analyzed relationship-related phrases such as:

```text
Rachel and Dana are in a romantic relationship.

Character A and Character B are lovers.

Character X is dating Character Y.
```

Using the detected names and gender information, the script inferred potential LGBTQ+ identities based on the relationship context.

```{python}
character_gender = "female"
partner_genders = ["female"]

if character_gender == "female" and "female" in partner_genders:
    print("Lesbian")
```

For example, when two female characters were identified as being romantically involved, the system could infer a Lesbian label. Similarly, relationships between two male characters could contribute to a Gay classification.

This additional inference step increased the number of automatically classified characters and reduced the number of entries that required manual review.

<blockquote>
The relationship inference process was particularly useful for descriptions that focused on relationships rather than explicitly mentioning sexual orientation or gender identity.
</blockquote>

</div>

# Manual Review {#manual-review}
<div class="manual-review-card">

Despite the automated classification process, some character descriptions remained ambiguous or lacked sufficient information for reliable labeling.

When the system could not confidently assign a label, the character was marked as:

```{python}
found_labels = []

if not found_labels:
    print("[MANUAL_REVIEW_REQUIRED]")
```

These entries were exported into a separate review file and manually inspected.

During this step, additional information from the original source material was used to assign the most appropriate LGBTQ+ identity label.

<blockquote>
The manual review stage served as a quality assurance mechanism and helped improve the overall accuracy of the dataset.
</blockquote>

After the review process was completed, the manually labeled records were merged with the automatically classified entries to create the final dataset used for analysis and visualization.

This combination of automated processing and manual validation resulted in a more complete and reliable dataset.
</div>

# Load Final Dataset {#load-final-dataset}

<div class="dataset-card">

<p>
After completing the automatic labeling and manual review process, the final dataset was loaded for analysis and visualization.
</p>

<blockquote>
The final dataset combines automatically classified entries with manually reviewed records.
</blockquote>

<p>
Dataset used for the final analysis:
</p>

<pre><code>final_labeled_data.csv</code></pre>

<p>
The table below shows the first rows of the dataset used throughout the analysis.
</p>

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

df = pd.read_csv(
    "../data/processed/final_labeled_data.csv",
    sep=None,
    engine="python"
)

df.head()
```
</div>


# Dataset Overview {#dataset-overview}
<div class="overview-card">

<p>
After data cleaning, enrichment, and manual review, the final dataset was prepared for analysis.
</p>

<blockquote>
The dataset contains LGBTQ+ characters from dramatic television series released during the 2020s.
</blockquote>

<p>
Each row represents a single television character together with contextual information about the series and the detected LGBTQ+ identity label.
</p>

<p>
The final dataset includes the following variables:
</p>

<ul>
  <li>Year</li>
  <li>Series</li>
  <li>Network</li>
  <li>Character</li>
  <li>Actor</li>
  <li>Notes</li>
  <li>Final_Label</li>
</ul>

<p>
The <code>Final_Label</code> column contains automatically or manually assigned LGBTQ+ identity categories.
</p>

<p>
The following Python code provides a basic overview of the dataset structure.
</p>

```{python}
print(f"Rows: {len(df)}")
print(f"Columns: {len(df.columns)}")

for col in df.columns:
    print("•", col)
```
</div>

# Distribution of LGBTQ+ Identities {#distribution-of-lgbtq-identities}

<div class="distribution-card">

<blockquote>
Multiple identity labels were split into individual categories and counted separately to provide a clearer overview of LGBTQ+ representation.
</blockquote>

The following visualization shows the distribution of LGBTQ+ identity labels within the final dataset.

Because some characters were associated with multiple identity labels, the labels were separated and counted individually in the analysis.

This approach provides a clearer overview of how frequently different LGBTQ+ identities appear across the television series included in the dataset.

The following code splits multiple labels, removes unresolved entries, and calculates the total frequency of each identity category.

```{python}
labels = (
    df["Final_Label"]
    .dropna()
    .astype(str)
    .str.split(";")
    .explode()
    .str.strip()
)

labels = labels[
    labels != "[MANUAL_REVIEW_REQUIRED]"
]

counts = labels.value_counts()

counts_df = (
    counts
    .reset_index()
    .rename(columns={
        "index": "Identity",
        "Final_Label": "Count"
    })
)

counts_df
```
</div>

# Treemap / Overview {#treemap-overview}

<div class="viz-card">

![](../visualizations/identity_distribution_overview.png)

</div>

# LGBTQ+ Representation Over Time {#lgbtq-representation-over-time}

<div class="viz-card">

![](../visualizations/identity_representation_over_time_stacked.png)

</div>

# Development Across Years {#development-across-years}

<div class="viz-card">

![](../visualizations/identity_development_over_years.png)

</div>

# Key Findings {#key-findings}

<div class="findings-card">

<p>
The analysis revealed several important patterns regarding LGBTQ+ representation in dramatic television series of the 2020s.
</p>

<blockquote>
Three key findings emerged from the dataset and visualizations.
</blockquote>

<h3>1. Gay and Lesbian Representation Dominates</h3>

<p>
The majority of characters identified in the dataset were classified as either Gay or Lesbian. These two categories account for the largest share of LGBTQ+ representation across the analyzed television series.
</p>

<p>
This suggests that television productions continue to focus primarily on identities that have historically received the greatest visibility within mainstream media.
</p>

<h3>2. Representation Increased Throughout the 2020s</h3>

<p>
The temporal visualizations indicate a general increase in the number of LGBTQ+ characters represented over the years.
</p>

<p>
Streaming platforms and international productions appear to contribute significantly to this development, resulting in greater LGBTQ+ visibility in contemporary television.
</p>

<h3>3. Uneven Representation Across Identity Groups</h3>

<p>
While Gay and Lesbian identities were strongly represented, other LGBTQ+ identities appeared considerably less frequently.
</p>

<p>
Categories such as Non-binary, Intersex, Genderfluid, and Asexual accounted for only a small proportion of the dataset.
</p>

<p>
This suggests that LGBTQ+ representation has expanded, but diversity within LGBTQ+ representation remains uneven.
</p>

<blockquote>
Overall, the findings indicate growing LGBTQ+ visibility in television while also highlighting continuing disparities between different identity groups.
</blockquote>

</div>

# Interpretation of Results {#interpretation-of-results}

<div class="interpretation-card">

<p>
The visualization shows that gay and lesbian characters represent the largest proportion of LGBTQ+ identities within the dataset.
</p>

<blockquote>
Gay and lesbian identities account for the majority of LGBTQ+ character representations in dramatic television series of the 2020s.
</blockquote>

<p>
This suggests that television series continue to focus primarily on gay and lesbian representation, while other LGBTQ+ identities appear less frequently.
</p>

<p>
Identities such as:
</p>

<ul>
  <li>Non-binary</li>
  <li>Intersex</li>
  <li>Genderfluid</li>
  <li>Asexual</li>
</ul>

<p>
appear significantly less often in comparison.
</p>

<p>
One possible explanation is that mainstream television productions still rely more heavily on identities that are already more socially visible and widely represented in media culture.
</p>

<p>
The dataset also indicates that streaming platforms such as Netflix, HBO, Hulu, and Amazon Prime Video contribute strongly to LGBTQ+ representation in television series.
</p>

<p>
Many of the analyzed series originated from streaming services rather than traditional television networks.
</p>

<p>
Another important observation is that several characters were associated with multiple identity categories, demonstrating that LGBTQ+ identities are often complex and cannot always be reduced to a single label.
</p>

<p>
Overall, the analysis suggests that LGBTQ+ visibility in television has increased during the 2020s, but representation remains uneven across different identity groups.
</p>

</div>

# Methodological Reflection {#methodological-reflection}

<div class="reflection-card">

<p>
This project used a rule-based classification approach instead of machine learning methods.
</p>

<blockquote>
The primary goal was to ensure transparency, reproducibility, and interpretability throughout the enrichment process.
</blockquote>

<p>
The enrichment process relied primarily on:
</p>

<ul>
  <li>keyword matching</li>
  <li>regular expressions (regex)</li>
  <li>relationship inference</li>
  <li>manual validation</li>
</ul>

<p>
One advantage of this approach is that the classification process can be reproduced consistently across the dataset.
</p>

<p>
In addition, the method does not require large training datasets or complex machine learning models.
</p>

<p>
However, the approach also has several weaknesses.
</p>

<p>
Keyword-based systems depend heavily on the wording used in the Notes column. If identities or relationships are described indirectly, the system may fail to classify the character correctly.
</p>

<p>
For example, descriptions such as:
</p>

<pre><code>
"They are romantically involved."
</code></pre>

<p>
provide limited contextual information and may require manual interpretation.
</p>

</div>

# Limitations {#limitations}

<div class="limitations-grid">

<div class="limitation-card">

<blockquote>
Dependence on Wikipedia
</blockquote>

<p>
The dataset was based entirely on information collected from Wikipedia.
</p>

<p>
Because Wikipedia is community-generated, the data may contain inconsistencies, incomplete entries, or outdated information.
</p>

<p>
Some television characters also had very limited descriptions, which made automatic classification more difficult.
</p>

</div>

<div class="limitation-card">

<blockquote>
Ambiguous Character Descriptions
</blockquote>

<p>
Many entries in the Notes column described relationships indirectly without explicitly mentioning LGBTQ+ identities.
</p>

<p>
For example, descriptions such as:
</p>

<pre><code>
"X and Y are in a relationship."
</code></pre>

<p>
provide insufficient information for reliable automatic classification and often require manual review.
</p>

</div>

</div>

# Conclusion {#conclusion}

<div class="conclusion-card">

<p>
This project explored LGBTQ+ representation in dramatic television series from the 2020s using computational data analysis methods.
</p>

<blockquote>
The project demonstrates how computational approaches can support research in media studies and gender studies.
</blockquote>

<p>
The project combined multiple stages of data processing, including:
</p>

<ul>
  <li>data extraction</li>
  <li>data cleaning</li>
  <li>automated label detection</li>
  <li>relationship inference</li>
  <li>manual review</li>
  <li>data visualization</li>
</ul>

<p>
The results showed that gay and lesbian characters are represented more frequently than other LGBTQ+ identities such as non-binary, intersex, genderfluid, or asexual characters.
</p>

<p>
The analysis also demonstrated the important role of streaming platforms in increasing LGBTQ+ visibility within contemporary television media.
</p>

<p>
At the same time, the project highlighted several methodological challenges, including ambiguous descriptions, incomplete source data, and the limitations of automated identity classification.
</p>

<p>
By combining automated methods with manual validation, the project achieved a more reliable and reproducible dataset.
</p>

<blockquote>
Overall, the project demonstrates how computational approaches can be applied to questions in media studies and gender studies while also emphasizing the importance of critical reflection when working with social and cultural data.
</blockquote>

</div>


# Reproducibility {#reproducibility}
<div class="reproducibility-card">

One of the main objectives of this project was to ensure that the entire workflow could be reproduced by other researchers.

The project follows a structured data pipeline that transforms raw Wikipedia data into a cleaned, enriched, and analyzed dataset. All processing steps were implemented in Python and documented within the GitHub repository.

The workflow can be reproduced by executing the scripts in the following order:

1. `data_extraction.py`
   - Extracts the original table from Wikipedia and stores it as a CSV file.

2. `data_enrichment.py`
   - Performs the initial LGBTQ+ identity detection based on predefined keyword patterns.

3. `data_enrichment_plus.py`
   - Applies additional enrichment methods, including relationship inference and gender-based classification.

4. `manual_review_needed.csv`
   - Contains entries that could not be classified automatically and therefore required manual validation.

5. `merge_final_data.py`
   - Combines automatically classified records with manually reviewed entries to create the final dataset.

6. `advanced_visualizations.py`
   - Generates the visualizations used throughout the analysis.

7. `report.qmd`
   - Produces the final report using Quarto.

All required Python packages are listed in the `requirements.txt` file, allowing the computational environment to be recreated easily.

The final dataset used for analysis is stored as:

```text
data/processed/final_labeled_data.csv
```

The complete project is managed using Git and GitHub, which enables version control, collaboration, and transparency throughout the development process.

By combining Python scripts, structured datasets, version control through GitHub, and Quarto reporting, the project provides a transparent and reproducible workflow from data collection to final analysis.

</div>

# References {#references}

<div class="references-card">

<p class="source-label">Data Source</p>

<p class="source-value">
<a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_dramatic_television_series_with_LGBTQ_characters:_2020s" target="_blank">
Wikipedia ↗
</a>
</p>

<p class="source-label">Knowledge Base</p>

<p class="source-value">
<a href="https://www.wikidata.org/" target="_blank">
Wikidata ↗
</a>
</p>

<p class="source-label">Data Analysis</p>

<p class="source-value">
<a href="https://pandas.pydata.org/" target="_blank">
pandas Documentation ↗
</a>
</p>

<p class="source-value">
<a href="https://matplotlib.org/" target="_blank">
matplotlib Documentation ↗
</a>
</p>

<p class="source-label">Report Framework</p>

<p class="source-value">
<a href="https://quarto.org/" target="_blank">
Quarto Documentation ↗
</a>
</p>

<p class="source-label">Additional Libraries</p>

<p class="source-value">
<a href="https://pypi.org/project/gender-guesser/" target="_blank">
gender-guesser ↗
</a>
</p>

<p class="source-value">
<a href="https://www.python.org/" target="_blank">
Python Programming Language ↗
</a>
</p>

</div>

<script src="scrollspy.js"></script>
